frame_analyses.description: Setup view — Penrose drain on red foam suture block. Drain slit visible running longitudinally. No instruments in field yet.
At 9 seconds both instruments are already in the field and actively engaged near the drain. Needle and suture visible. Instruments positioned close to the drain surface immediately.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Quick entry — instruments engaged with the task within seconds. Notably faster setup than typical novice performance. Minimal idle time.

frame_analyses.description: At 26 seconds, both instruments working near the drain with needle being driven through the tissue. Close instrument coordination visible.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Needle driving in progress. Instruments are close together indicating focused work near the marks. The trainee appears more confident in needle handling than in the pre-training video.

frame_analyses.description: At 52 seconds, both instruments still working at the drain surface. Suture thread visible between instruments. Completing passage through second mark.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture placement still taking considerable time (~45 seconds), though improved from pre-training baseline. Instruments are working with more purpose and less apparent fumbling.

frame_analyses.description: At 70 seconds, clear wrapping motion visible. The right instrument (silver/gray) has suture looped around its tip in a double-wrap pattern. Left instrument holding the free end near the drain. Classic surgeon's knot wrapping setup.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The wrapping motion is clearly visible — suture loops around the right instrument consistent with a double throw (surgeon's knot). The technique appears more deliberate and controlled than the pre-training video.

frame_analyses.description: At 87 seconds, instruments near drain surface tightening the first throw. Both instruments engaged close to the drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Tightening the surgeon's knot. Working close to the tissue surface which helps maintain knot tension.

frame_analyses.description: At 105 seconds, clear wrapping for second throw. The LEFT instrument now has suture wrapped around it, with right instrument holding the free end. Large suture loop visible above the drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: HAND SWITCH CONFIRMED — in the first throw, the right instrument led the wrap. Now the left instrument is leading. This indicates proper hand exchange between throws as required by FLS protocol.

frame_analyses.description: At 131 seconds, instruments working near drain. Suture visible. Drain slit edges appear to be closing — better approximation visible compared to earlier frames.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Third throw in progress or being tightened. The drain slit closure appears improved — edges are coming together more completely.

frame_analyses.description: At 157 seconds, instrument shape near the drain appears different — consistent with endoscopic scissors. Suture loop visible above instrument. Cutting of first suture end appears in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture cutting phase. The instrument profile is thinner/different from the needle drivers, consistent with scissors introduction.

frame_analyses.description: At 166 seconds, long suture tail visible extending from drain toward upper left. Instrument working near drain cutting the second end.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting second suture end. The long tail visible suggests the first cut was completed and the second is in progress.

frame_analyses.description: Final frame at 175 seconds. Instruments withdrawn. Completed knot visible on the Penrose drain. The drain slit shows IMPROVED CLOSURE compared to the pre-training video — edges are more closely approximated with only a minimal residual gap. Short suture tails visible near the knot. Drain remains securely attached to suture block.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Significantly improved final result compared to pre-training baseline. Better slit closure, clean knot visible, suture tails appropriately cut. Drain not avulsed.

knot_assessments.notes: Frame 9 clearly shows double-loop wrapping around right instrument — consistent with surgeon's knot. Wrapping motion is more confident than pre-training video. Confidence moderate-high.
Frame 13 clearly shows LEFT instrument leading the wrap (vs RIGHT in throw 1). Hand switch confirmed. Single loop wrapping pattern visible. Confidence moderate.

Third throw completed by frame 16. Final result shows good slit closure, suggesting the third throw was adequately secured. Cannot confirm hand switch direction from available frames. Confidence low-moderate.

technique_summary: This post-practice video shows clear improvement over the pre-training baseline. Completion time decreased from ~198 seconds to ~170 seconds (28 second improvement). Needle loading time halved from 30 seconds to approximately 15 seconds. The most significant improvement is in the final result — the drain slit shows complete or near-complete closure versus the visible gap in the pre-training video, indicating better knot tension and/or more precise suture placement. Hand switching between throws appears to have occurred, with frame 13 clearly showing the wrapping hand changed from right (throw 1) to left (throw 2). All three throws were completed and the drain was not avulsed. The primary remaining area for improvement is suture placement speed (still ~40 seconds) and overall throw execution time.
improvement_suggestions: Continue reducing suture placement time — current ~40 seconds can be improved to 25-30 seconds with more practice on perpendicular needle driving
Work on throw speed — each throw still takes 25-30 seconds, with experienced practitioners completing each in 15-20 seconds
Practice cutting technique — the suture cut phase took ~25 seconds which can be reduced to 10-15 seconds
Focus on economy of motion during transitions between phases to shave additional seconds
strengths: Significant time improvement from pre-training baseline (198s → 170s, a 14% reduction)
Needle loading time halved compared to pre-training (30s → 15s)
Excellent drain slit closure — marked improvement from the visible gap in the pre-training video
Proper surgeon's knot for first throw with visible double-loop wrapping
Hand switch confirmed between throw 1 and throw 2
Drain not avulsed — maintained appropriate tissue handling throughout
All three throws completed in correct sequence

key_improvements: Overall confidence in knot security improved
